sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 7, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 6 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have a much clearer picture. The 2x2 blocks are:
- Divider white blocks at cols 32-33, rows: 10-11, 14-15, 18-19, 22-23, 26-27, 30-31, 34-35, 38-39, 42-43, 46-47, 50-51
- Yellow block at rows 20-21, cols 24-25
- Green dot (N) at rows 40-41, cols 10-11
The divider has gaps between the white blocks. The gaps are at rows 12-13, 16-17, 20-21, 24-25, 28-29, 32-33, 36-37, 40-41, 44-45, 48-49.
Looking at the right side shapes:
- Red shape at roughly rows 12-15, cols 40-47 (white frame with red fill)
- Blue shape at roughly rows 36-39, cols 40-47 (white frame with blue fill)
Now the key insight: when the green dot is at the right edge of the gray block and presses UP, it swaps with a divider block. When it presses DOWN from the divider, it swaps back.
Let me think about what the goal might be:
- The green dot can swap with divider blocks
- The right side has shapes with colored fills
- Maybe the goal is to move colored blocks from the left through the divider gaps to the right side?
Wait, let me reconsider. The green dot is inside a gray block. When it moves UP from the gray block, it swaps with a divider block. This means the divider block moves into the gray block. What if the goal is to fill the right-side shapes by moving colored blocks through the gaps?
Let me look at the left side more carefully:
- Blue block (2x2) at some position
- Yellow block (2x2) at rows 20-21, cols 24-25
- Red/dark red blocks
- Blue pattern (checkerboard)
- Gray block with green dot
Maybe the green dot needs to navigate to specific positions to "collect" colors and then deposit them on the right side?
Let me try a different approach: move the green dot to the right edge of the gray block, then try UP to swap with the divider, and then RIGHT to see if it can go through the gap to the right side.
Actually, I need to reconsider the mechanics. Let me trace through what happened:
1. Initial: green dot at rows 40-41, cols 10-11 (inside gray block)
2. RIGHT: green dot moved to cols 12-13 (still inside gray block)
3. UP: green dot swapped to rows 38-39, cols 32-33 (divider)
4. RIGHT: no impact (can't swap with black)
5. DOWN: green dot swapped back to rows 40-41, cols 12-13 (gray block)
6. LEFT: green dot moved to cols 10-11 (inside gray block)
So the green dot can move within the gray block (LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN) and can also swap with the divider when at the right edge and pressing UP.
Let me try: move to right edge (RIGHT), then UP to swap to divider, then try to navigate along the divider to a gap position.
Actually, I realize I need to understand the divider structure better. The white blocks are at specific rows. Between them are gaps. The green dot, when on the divider, can move UP/DOWN to swap with adjacent divider blocks or gaps.
Let me try: RIGHT (to move to right edge of gray block), UP (swap to divider), then UP again to see if it moves along the divider.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try RIGHT then UP to get back to the divider, then explore further
# Green dot is at rows 40-41, cols 10-11

# First RIGHT to move to cols 12-13
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print("RIGHT result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 34:
        print(f"Green dot: {n['boundary']}")

# Now UP to swap to divider
result = action(['UP'])
print("\nUP result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 34:
        print(f"Green dot after UP: {n['boundary']}")

# Now try UP again to see if it moves along the divider
result = action(['UP'])
print("\nUP again result:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'), last_action_result.get('no_impact'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 34:
        print(f"Green dot after 2nd UP: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
RIGHT result: True False
Green dot: [[40, 12], [40, 13], [41, 13], [41, 12]]

UP result: True False
Green dot after UP: [[40, 12], [40, 13], [41, 13], [41, 12]]

UP again result: True False
Green dot after 2nd UP: [[38, 32], [38, 33], [39, 33], [39, 32]]
